characters.blurb: King of Athens who left tokens under a stone at Troezen. He later recognized Theseus by the sword, then leapt to his death when the tribute ship returned without the white sail.
Daughter of Pittheus and mother of Theseus. She revealed his true parentage and later was taken with Helen when Castor and Pollux recovered their sister.
Wise ruler of Troezen who raised Theseus and steered Aegeus into Aethra’s bed by reading the Delphic oracle to his own advantage.
Early king of Attica from whom Theseus’s paternal house claimed descent—a pedigree Menestheus and the Pallantidae later disputed.

characters.relation: Uncle and cousins; rivals for Athens
characters.blurb: Pallas and his fifty sons opposed Aegeus and then Theseus. Their ambush failed after Leos betrayed them; the survivors fled.
Fled Corinth to Aegeus and, fearing a rival heir, nearly had Theseus poisoned at a banquet before Aegeus knew his son.
The hero Theseus most admired and emulated. Related through Pelops’s line, he later freed Theseus from Aidoneus’s prison in Epirus.
Club-bearer near Epidaurus, also called Corynetes. Theseus killed him in combat and kept the club as Hercules kept the lion’s skin.
Robber who killed travelers by bending pines. Theseus destroyed him the same way and later took his daughter Perigune.

characters.relation: Cliff robber on the Megarian border
characters.blurb: Forced strangers to wash his feet, then kicked them into the sea. Theseus cast him down the same rocks; Megarians later disputed the tale.
Arcadian who challenged passers-by at Eleusis. Theseus beat him at his own sport and killed him.
Also called Damastes: he forced guests to fit his bed. Theseus fitted him to it in turn, in the style of Hercules’s reprisals.
Elderly woman who sheltered young Theseus before the Marathonian bull. He later honored her with the Hecalesia rites.

characters.relation: King of Crete; imposed the tribute
characters.blurb: Cretan king who warred on Athens after Androgeus’s death and exacted the seven youths and seven maidens. Athenian tragedy blackened his name.

characters.relation: Son of Minos; his death sparked the tribute
characters.blurb: Murdered in Attica; Minos’s revenge and the gods’ plague drove Athens into the deadly Cretan tribute.
In the poetic tale, a bull-man in the labyrinth. Some Cretans say “Taurus” was a cruel games champion; either way, Theseus ended the tribute.

characters.relation: Daughter of Minos; helper, then deserted
characters.blurb: Gave Theseus the clue through the labyrinth and sailed with him. Stories differ—desertion, Naxos, Cyprus—but she is central to his Cretan fame.
Famed artisan who fled Crete to Athens. Theseus claimed him as cousin-german and, in one tradition, used him as a guide against Crete.
Amazon taken (or given) in the Euxine expedition. Historians call her Antiope or Hippolyta; she bore Hippolytus and died in the Amazon war on Attica.

characters.relation: Later wife; mother of the tragic house
characters.blurb: Married after Antiope’s death. Plutarch leaves her calamities with Hippolytus to the tragic poets, who agree on the disaster.
Son of Theseus and Antiope. His ruin with Phaedra is the stuff of Attic tragedy; Plutarch will not contradict the dramatists.
Lapith prince who tested Theseus by cattle-raiding, then swore friendship. They fought the Centaurs together and later stole Helen—and tried for Aidoneus’s daughter.

characters.relation: Abducted as a child; cause of his fall
characters.blurb: Taken from Sparta with Pirithous while still too young to marry, then hidden at Aphidnae. Her brothers’ war opened Athens to Menestheus.
Recovered Helen from Aphidnae, were welcomed by Menestheus, and honored at Athens as Anaces. Their war sealed Theseus’s political ruin.
Descendant of Erechtheus who courted the people against Theseus, aided Castor and Pollux, and held the kingdom until he died in the Trojan war.
Told Castor and Pollux that Helen was at Aphidnae. The Academy was later spared by Spartans for his sake.

characters.relation: Ally who kept Helen at Aphidnae
characters.blurb: Trusted with Helen (and Aethra) at Aphidnae. He later adopted Castor and Pollux when they were initiated at Athens.
King in Epirus who styled his household after the underworld. He threw Pirithous to his dog and jailed Theseus until Hercules begged his release.

characters.relation: King of Scyros; agent of his death
characters.blurb: Host on Scyros who—jealous, or to please Menestheus—hurled Theseus from a cliff. Others say Theseus slipped after supper.
Son of Chalcodon in Euboea; Theseus sent his children to him before sailing to Scyros. They later went with him to Troy.

characters.relation: Later recovered his bones for Athens
characters.blurb: Athenian general who took Scyros and, guided by an eagle, found Theseus’s coffin and brought the relics home in state.
Troezen’s chief god; Pittheus spread the story that Neptune, not Aegeus, had begotten Theseus—a claim that fed his heroic prestige.

characters.relation: Love for whom he deserted Ariadne
characters.blurb: Daughter of Panopeus. One tradition says desire for her made Theseus leave Ariadne—a charge Plutarch calls neither just nor honorable.
The savage Crommyonian sow—or, in another telling, a cruel woman nicknamed Sow—whom Theseus sought out and killed.

characters.relation: Daughter of Sinnis; bore him Melanippus
characters.blurb: Hid among brush after her father’s death, then came out on Theseus’s promise. She bore him Melanippus and later married Deioneus.

Son of Aegeus (or Neptune) who cleared the roads of brigands, killed the Minotaur, and united Attica into one city under one law. His loyalty to Pirithous and the abduction of Helen brought his final ruin, yet Athens honoured him as hero and protector for centuries.
Athenian chronicler whose account of the tribute and Theseus's age Plutarch weighs against Homer and the tragedians when the sources multiply and conflict.
Lesbos historian who recorded an alternative account of Ariadne's companions on Naxos—one of the sources Plutarch sifts to find a fair reading of the abandonment.
Tantalid hero from whom Pittheus descended; the shared pedigree made Theseus and Hercules cousin-german and gave the younger hero a heroic model to emulate and surpass.
In the Iliad's catalogue of ships Menestheus commands Athens, not Theseus—an omission Plutarch reads as Homer's political sympathy for the usurper who displaced the founder.
Alongside Homer among the earliest poets Plutarch consults when tracing heroic lineage and the myths surrounding Minos and the tribute of Athens.
His Constitution of the Bottiaeans is cited as evidence that the tribute children went as slaves, not sacrifices—a scholarly tradition Plutarch sets beside the tragic accounts.
Cean lyric poet whose account of Ariadne's fate Plutarch compares with others, finding the versions irreconcilable but preserving each as a window on older tradition.
Plutarch defers to him and the other tragic poets for the Phaedra catastrophe, remarking that all the dramatists agree on the disaster even if they differ on its cause.
The Pythian oracle shadowed Aegeus's visit to Delphi before Theseus's conception and Theseus's own dedications when he returned victorious from Crete.
From Heracleia; recorded a version in which Theseus received Antiope as a peaceful gift from her people rather than seizing her—one of several accounts Plutarch cannot adjudicate.
Theseus's son by the daughter of Sinnis. His descendants settled among Ionians in Asia who, for Perigune's sake, held the asparagus plant sacred and never burned it.

characters.relation: Ancestral figure linking Theseus to Pelops
characters.blurb: Daughter of Pelops named in one genealogy as kin of Pittheus—the lineage that placed Theseus inside the great heroic pedigree and justified his emulation of Hercules.

characters.relation: Tragedian cited on the Cretan myths
characters.blurb: Invoked alongside Euripides as a tragic authority whose treatment of Minos and the tribute Plutarch reads when weighing the differing poetic versions of the story.

characters.relation: God who consoled Ariadne on Naxos
characters.blurb: In the tradition that exonerates Theseus, Dionysus found and loved Ariadne on Naxos—making the desertion almost a divine bequest rather than a cold betrayal.
In the tradition that separates Ariadne's story cleanly from Phaedra's, Deucalion struck a treaty with Theseus and bestowed Phaedra in marriage as its seal.
Revealed the conspiracy of Pallas and his sons to Theseus, turning the planned ambush into a rout. The Attic deme Leontis preserved his name in civic memory.
Steered the tribute ship to Crete alongside Phaeax; both pilots were afterward honored at the Kyberneseia festival Theseus instituted in Athens.

characters.relation: Second pilot of the Cretan voyage
characters.blurb: Shared the helm with Nausithous on the passage to Crete; the Kyberneseia rites Theseus founded recalled both pilots' faithful service.

characters.relation: Salaminian soothsayer who sent pilots to Theseus
characters.blurb: Diviner from Salamis who provided Theseus with Nausithous as steersman and Phaeax as look-out man for the Cretan voyage—distinct from the robber Sciron.

characters.relation: Amazon-voyage companion who died for love
characters.blurb: One of Theseus's companions on the Euxine expedition; fell in love with Antiope, confided in a friend, and drowned himself—the death that bound Theseus to the Amazon by pity.

characters.relation: Son of Sciron; slain at Aphidnae
characters.blurb: Sciron's son who died fighting on the Dioscuri's side when they recovered Helen from Aphidnae—his tomb was pointed out on the Megarian plain.
Theseus instituted the Isthmian games in honor of Neptune but also kept Jupiter's rites; the paean and olive branch belonged to Jupiter Hecaleius.

characters.relation: God whose image stood at the city gate
characters.blurb: A figure of Hermes at Aegeus's gate and an honored shrine in the Amazon Quarter recalled the god's connection to Theseus's family.

characters.relation: Goddess Theseus invoked before the Cretan voyage
characters.blurb: The oracle at Delphi told Theseus to make Venus his guide and invoke her as companion before sailing to Crete; her grove at Naxos later received Ariadne.

characters.relation: Roman pair in the Parallel Lives
characters.blurb: Romulus is paired with Theseus—both founders born out of wedlock, both men who slew kinsmen and lost their thrones through bad deeds.

locations.blurb: Raised at Pittheus’s court in Troezen—Aethra’s home—before the road of labors toward Athens.

locations.relation: First labor on the Isthmus road
locations.blurb: Where he met Periphetes the Club-bearer and took the weapon that marked his clearing of the road.
Along the Saronic shore he slew the Crommyonian sow—another terror cleared for travelers to Athens.
Sciron’s cliff road above the sea—Theseus hurled the robber down the way he had cast strangers.
At Eleusis he overthrew Cercyon in wrestling—then Procrustes farther on—before entering Attica as hero.

locations.relation: City he claimed and later ruled
locations.blurb: Recognized by the sword and sandals, he entered Athens—heir of Aegeus, founder-figure of the democracy’s myth.
He captured the Marathonian bull and drove it to Athens—Attica’s countryside made safe in the same spirit as the Isthmus road.
Sailed as tribute to Minos; with Ariadne’s thread he entered the Labyrinth and slew the Minotaur.
On the voyage home Ariadne was left at Naxos—love and desertion tangled in the same island stop.
He put in at Delos to dance the Crane and thank Apollo—vow and thanksgiving after Crete.
Far north on the Black Sea shore he fought Amazons and took Hippolyta—war that later returned to Attica.
He hid young Helen at Aphidnae; the Dioscuri stormed it—Attica paid for the abduction.
Exiled to Scyros, he fell—or was cast—from the cliff by Lycomedes; later Cimon brought his bones home.
The Isthmus corridor of his youthful labors—Corinth’s land between two gulfs, gateway toward Athens.

locations.relation: Home of Helen and the Dioscuri
locations.blurb: Helen’s city—her seizure at Aphidnae drew Castor and Pollux north against Theseus’s Attica.
Named among Greek lands around Attica’s politics and wars in the Theseus cycle.
Far shore of myth—paired in Greek story with Black Sea voyages beyond the Thermodon.
Plutarch pairs Theseus with Romulus—Athens’s founder-myth set beside Rome’s.
